content: For administering both CAR-T cell treatments, it is important to confirm the patient’s identity with the patient identifiers on the infusion bag, as they are all for autologous use. A leuodepleting filter cannot be used, and central venous access is the recommended method for infusion. Interleukin-6 antagonists (ie, tocilizumab or siltuximab), corticosteroids, and emergent equipment are needed before infusion and during the recovery period in case of side effects.

title: Indium-111 White Blood Cell Scan -- Specimen Collection
content: The preparation of In-111–labeled leukocytes for diagnostic imaging requires strict adherence to aseptic techniques and established protocols. The following provides an overview of the procedure based on established guidelines. [3] [8] However, clinicians should always consult the latest recommendations when preparing imaging protocols. The process begins with collecting 40 to 80 mL of blood from adult patients (10–15 mL for pediatric patients) using at least a 20G needle to minimize cell damage. [8] Blood is drawn into a sterile container containing acid-citrate-dextrose (ACD) formulation A per European Pharmacopoeia) or heparin as an anticoagulant. Leukopenia is a relative contraindication due to reduced WBC labeling efficiency.

title: Blood Donation -- Indications
content: Donating and storing umbilical cord blood provides an excellent source for hematopoeitic stem cell transplantation. Cord blood has a significantly higher percentage of hematopoietic stem cells than non-cord blood. Cord blood is collected during the delivery of a newborn and processed and stored in blood banks specifically designed for cord blood, ensuring its availability for future use. [10]

content: The use of appropriate anticoagulants is crucial. Heparin is commonly used because it effectively prevents coagulation without significantly affecting the measurements of common electrolytes, as it can bind to positive ions. Blood samples must be thoroughly mixed immediately after collection to ensure uniform dispersion of the anticoagulant and to prevent sedimentation of cells. [10] Air bubbles: Air bubbles in the sample can falsely elevate pO2 readings. title: Exchange Transfusion -- Technique or Treatment -- Automated Exchange Transfusion
content: Clinicians program automated exchange machines to achieve the desired post-procedure HCT and hemoglobin S levels. During an automated exchange, patients have either 2 large peripheral intravenous catheters or 1 dual-lumen central venous catheter that allows the simultaneous withdrawal and return of blood. The catheter connects to sterile tubing in an apheresis machine, which separates the patient's blood into components. The machine removes a portion of the patient's RBCs and replaces them with donor RBCs.

title: Exchange Transfusion -- Equipment
content: Clinicians can perform RBC exchange transfusions manually or by using an automated apheresis machine; manual transfusions are necessary for very small patients. Automated machines are highly effective as they have settings to achieve the desired hemoglobin level. Patients require 2 sets of access points for drawing and returning blood. Further, double-lumen central catheters can be placed in the femoral vein in emergencies.

title: Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation -- Technique or Treatment -- Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Hematopoietic Stem Cells
content: The most important factors affecting the success of cord blood transplant are the total nucleated cell dose and HLA matching; the recommended minimum dose of total nucleated cells for successful engraftment is 2 x 10^7 cells/kg. Theoretically, strict HLA matching is not required in the case of cord blood transplant as cord blood is devoid of mature T cells, but studies have shown better outcomes when matching recipients at HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C, and HLA-DRB1. [40] Given that a single cord blood unit might not contain the required amount of nucleated cells, a double cord transplant is used. However, only one cord blood transplant product will dominate within three months of infusion. Further, randomized controlled trials failed to show a significant difference in outcome, benefits, or risks between double cord blood and a single cord blood transplant. [41] [42]

title: Determining the Need for Blood Transfusion -- Function -- Cell Salvage
content: The Association of Anaesthetists guidelines make the following transfusion recommendations: Use cell salvage when it can be expected to reduce the likelihood of allogeneic (donor) red cell transfusion and/or severe postoperative anemia. Collection of blood for potential cell salvage (‘collect only’ mode) should be considered for surgical procedures where blood loss may exceed 500 ml (or > 10% of calculated total blood volume) in adult patients or > 8 mL/kg (> 10% of calculated total blood volume) in children weighing > 10 kg. [10]

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
content: Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions comprise several distinct agents, including O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethyl starch. These products are employed as volume expanders or oxygen‑carrying solutions in clinical settings where conventional blood transfusion is unavailable or unsuitable. While each agent belongs to the same therapeutic category, hydroxyethyl starch carries a specific safety limitation: it is contraindicated in patients with thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians to avoid exacerbating clot‑related conditions when selecting a blood substitute or plasma protein fraction for patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

The framework is labeled with the Arabic terms الفصادة الضوئية and فصادة البلازما, denoting sub‑classifications that connect light apheresis (الفصادة الضوئية) as an intermediate venous procedure between general venous interventions and plasma apheresis (فصادة البلازما). title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
content: Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions comprise several specific agents, including O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethylový škrob. O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin is a hemoglobin‑based oxygen carrier designed to transport oxygen in place of red blood cells. Dextran, a high‑molecular‑weight polysaccharide, functions as a plasma volume expander and colloid solution. Hydroxyethylový škrob, a modified starch polymer, is also employed as a plasma volume expander to maintain intravascular volume. Together, these three substances represent the primary members of the blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions category, providing alternatives for blood transfusion, volume resuscitation, and oxygen delivery in clinical care.

title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1369
content: Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions are classified as a type of blood and related products, and O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin is identified as a member of the blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions group.
